content.problemStatement:
Heatmaps display data in a 2D grid using colors to represent values. \
They're useful for showing patterns or correlations in complex datasets.

Check out the code given in the IDE. Some add-ons as compared to what we have learnt earlier
- Creating the heatmap:
   - plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6)) sets the figure size
   - plt.imshow(data, cmap='viridis') is the main function for creating the heatmap 
      - data is our input array
      - cmap='viridis' sets the color scheme (a blue-green-yellow colormap)
- Adding a colorbar
   - plt.colorbar() adds a colorbar to show the scale

### Task
Use the following monthly temperatures for 3 years to generate the corresponding heatmap.
```python
monthly_temps = [
    [5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 32, 31, 26, 21, 15, 8],  # Year 2020
    [6, 11, 16, 21, 26, 31, 33, 32, 27, 22, 16, 9],  # Year 2021
    [4, 9, 14, 19, 24, 29, 31, 30, 25, 20, 14, 7]    # Year 2022
]
```
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
